draft.introduction: Ephrem the Syrian (c. 306–373) is the greatest poet-theologian of the Syriac Christian tradition. Unlike his Greek and Latin contemporaries, Ephrem did his theology not in prose treatises but in metrical hymns (madrashe) designed to be sung by choirs in the liturgy. His Hymns on Faith address the mystery of the Incarnation with an intimacy and paradoxical boldness that philosophical prose rarely achieves. Hymn 10 contains Ephrem’s most celebrated statement of the “wonderful exchange”: God gave humanity divinity, and humanity gave God its nature. The symmetry is deliberate and shocking. Ephrem does not hedge or qualify; the exchange is presented as a completed transaction. This hymn became a touchstone for Syriac theology’s understanding of deification as a mutual gift between Creator and creature.

translation.text: He gave us divinity, we gave him humanity. He lowered himself to our level that he might raise us to his. He took on our likeness that he might give us his own.
translation.original_text: Yahb lan alahuta, yahbnan leh barnashuta. Etmakkakh lwathan d-nrawmnan lwatheh.

translator_notes.note: The Syriac original is presented in simplified transliteration. Ephrem’s term for divinity, alahuta, derives from the Syriac root for God (alaha) and carries the same concrete force as the Greek theotes. His term for humanity, barnashuta, literally means “son-of-man-ness”—the condition of being human. The parallelism of the Syriac is more compressed than any English rendering can convey: two short clauses, perfectly balanced, with the verbs mirroring each other. Ephrem’s theology of exchange influenced later Syriac authors and, through translations, fed into both Byzantine and Armenian theological traditions.
